entry.title: The Diplomat: 11 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • China’s Delivery Drone Rollout: Slow but Steady
  • Japan Struggles to Understand Abe Assassination
  • Sri Lankans Storm President’s House, Office in Biggest Rally Yet
  • Japan in Shock After Fatal Shooting of Former Prime Minister Abe Shinzo
  • Small State, Big Agenda? Nepal in the Gulf Labor Migration Market
  • The Impact and Implications of China’s Growing Influence in the Middle East
  • The Growing Tech Focus of the Quad
  • As Imran Khan Seeks a Comeback, All Eyes on By-Elections in Pakistan’s Punjab
  • China Reacts to Abe Shinzo Assassination
  • Child Rape Case Sparks Protests in Kyrgyzstan
  • Return of Industry and Loosening of Tech Restrictions Paves the Way for Chinese Stock Recovery
  • Wang’s G20 Meetings Highlight China’s Ukraine Messaging
  • Japan Ex-Leader Abe Shinzo Assassinated While Giving Speech
  • Mongolia: Wrestling With Modernization
  • China, South Korea Foreign Ministers Pledge Deepened Cooperation
  • Tokayev Looks to Caspian to Diversify Oil Export Routes
  • Japan Needs a National Space Strategy: Former JASDF General
  • ‘Hopeless’: Japan’s Weak Opposition No Match for Ruling LDP
  • India’s Double-Faced Approach to Terrorism
  • The Hong Kong Government and the Rule of Law
  • How to Fix China’s Population Crisis: Say Sorry to Women
  • Australia Drops Charge Against Lawyer Over Timor-Leste Spying Claim
  • Managing Cross-Regional Expectations After the NATO Summit
  • The China-Iran-Russia Triangle: Alternative World Order?
  • ASEAN Envoy Hails ‘Some Progress’ on Second Myanmar Mission
  • Foreign Casinos Have Been a Disaster for Cambodia
  • Reexamining East Malaysian Security in an Age of Growing Threats
  • Chinese Foreign Minister Courts Philippines’ President Marcos
  • Will the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act Make a Difference?
  • Japan’s NATO Outreach Bears Fruit
  • Ahead of G-20 Ministers’ Meeting, China Slams US, NATO
  • Can the Taliban Actually Prevent Attacks Launched From Afghan Soil?
  • South Korean Spy Agency Files Charges Against Ex-Chiefs Over North Korea Border Incidents
  • China’s Main Food Security Challenge: Feeding Its Pigs
  • Economic Crisis Forces Sri Lanka to Shed Fears and Move Closer to US, Middle East
  • Pipeline Critical to Kazakh Oil Exports Ordered to Halt Operations by Russian Court
  • Amid Worsening Medicine Shortages, North Korea Cracks Down on Private Drug Sellers
  • China Takes Full Advantage of Taliban’s Isolation
  • Twitter Files Lawsuit Against Indian Government in Court
  • Decapitation Video Points to Increasing Military Atrocities in Myanmar
  • Inflation in Laos Reaches 22-Year High as Economic Crisis Worsens
  • ‘Forward Singapore’ Offers a Timely Opportunity for Difficult Conversations
  • What Do High Oil Prices Mean for Clean Energy in Southeast Asia?
  • Campaigners Urge Clemency Ahead of Singapore Drug Executions
  • New Zealand Will Not Join the US Coalition Against China Anytime Soon
  • Russian Foreign Minister Visits Mongolia in Drive for Support
  • Unrest in Central Asia: The Trouble in Karakalpakstan
  • Is Jacinda Ardern Rethinking Her China Strategy?
  • Mongolia’s Military Diplomacy Highlights Female Peacekeepers
  • Can Japan’s Revamped Childcare Policy Get Workaholic Fathers to Take Leave?
  • Following NATO Summit, North Korea Denounces US-South Korea-Japan Joint Military Cooperation
  • China Sees Record Rains, Heat as Weather Turns Volatile
  • Karakalpak Protests Reflect the Shattered Promise of Mirziyoyev’s ‘New Uzbekistan’
  • In Kashmir, ‘Conscious Music’ Tests India’s Limits on Speech
  • Islamophobia Exists in India’s Private Sector
  • Russian Foreign Minister to Visit Vietnam Ahead of G-20
  • The Social Costs of Chinese Transnational Crime in Sihanoukville
  • Chinese FM Pledges ‘Closer’ Ties with Myanmar’s Military Government
  • Japan-South Korea Tensions on Display at NATO Summit
  • Afghan Clerics’ Assembly Urges Recognition of Taliban Government
  • Amid Shortages and Turmoil, Sri Lanka Grinds to a Halt
  • Russia-Ukraine War: Implications for Asian Geoeconomics
  • Modi’s UAE Stopover to Give a Fillip to the India-UAE Partnership
The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: The Atlantic: 10 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Lucky
  • Can You Cure Mental Illness? Two Centuries of Trying Says No.
  • Make Birth Free

  • The Domino Effects of New Anti-abortion Laws

  • What Trump Has Taken From Us

News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

ProPublica is an independent, nonprofit newsroom that produces investigative journalism with moral force.

entry.title: The Third Pole: 8 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 8 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-08 10:45AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

  • Who Are the Scientists Here?

  • Print Edition 43
  • Print Edition 42
  • Print Edition 41
  • Print Edition 40
  • Life Helps Make Almost Half of All Minerals on Earth
  • Under Anesthesia, Where Do Our Minds Go?
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • Don’t Give Up on Facts
  • Are You a Naïve Realist?
  • Moving Beyond Mimicry in Artificial Intelligence
  • Overthrowing the Patriarchy Through Ecstatic Sex
  • What Oceanographers Can Learn From Their Animal Colleagues
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

entry.title: Nature: 7 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Particle physics isn’t going to die — even if the LHC finds no new particles
  • Europe must not backslide on climate action despite war in Ukraine
  • Blanket bans on fossil fuels hurt women and lower-income countries
  • Painkillers are dispensed less freely by night-shift doctors
  • ‘Smart’ clothing flexes to provide relief from the heat

  • Extreme heat already claims lives in Latin American cities — and the toll is set to rise

  • Fast-evolving COVID variants complicate vaccine updates

  • The plastic paradox, and how to regulate the seas: Books in brief
  • To end coal, adapt to regional realities

  • Accelerate US development of syphilis self-tests
  • Promote clean-energy transition in student education
  • The conference challenges faced by scientists who stutter
  • Need web data? Here’s how to harvest them
  • Improving livestock to fight poverty and empower women
  • Cancer cells spread aggressively during sleep
  • Topology turns the crank on a magnetoelectric switch
  • From the archive: Shakespeare’s stage directions, and fern fever
  • Touch-evoked itch pinned on Piezo1 ion-channel protein
  • Synthetic molecular cluster hints at mechanism of nitrogen fixation
  • A cell atlas for migraine research
  • The Higgs boson turns ten
  • Turbulent cold flows gave birth to the first quasars
  • A detailed map of Higgs boson interactions by the ATLAS experiment ten years after the discovery
  • A portrait of the Higgs boson by the CMS experiment ten years after the discovery
  • Entangling single atoms over 33 km telecom fibre
  • Direct observation of vortices in an electron fluid
  • Topologically protected magnetoelectric switching in a multiferroic
  • Nitrogen reduction by the Fe sites of synthetic [Mo3S4Fe] cubes
  • Optimization of avian perching manoeuvres
  • Whole-genome sequencing reveals host factors underlying critical COVID-19
  • PIEZO1 transduces mechanical itch in mice
  • Biosynthetic potential of the global ocean microbiome
  • Characterization and antiviral susceptibility of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron BA.2
  • Increased memory B cell potency and breadth after a SARS-CoV-2 mRNA boost
  • cBAF complex components and MYC cooperate early in CD8+ T cell fate
  • Ablation of cDC2 development by triple mutations within the Zeb2 enhancer
  • Deciphering the immunopeptidome in vivo reveals new tumour antigens
  • The metastatic spread of breast cancer accelerates during sleep
  • GREM1 is required to maintain cellular heterogeneity in pancreatic cancer
  • OCA-T1 and OCA-T2 are coactivators of POU2F3 in the tuft cell lineage
  • Compatibility rules of human enhancer and promoter sequences
  • eIF5B and eIF1A reorient initiator tRNA to allow ribosomal subunit joining
  • Cryo-EM structure of a type IV secretion system
  • A path forward for analysing the impacts of marine protected areas
  • Reply to: A path forward for analysing the impacts of marine protected areas
  • Events Guide 2022
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.

  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022
entry.summary: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022 published at 2022-06-21 10:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • How Animals Perceive the World
  • Against Labels
  • American Rasputin
  • They Bent to Their Knees and Kissed the Sand
  • A Lost Trove of Civil War Gold, an FBI Excavation, and Some Very Angry Treasure Hunters
  • The Vindication of Jack White
  • We Have No Nuclear Strategy
  • My Daughter’s White Doll
  • The Book That Never Stops Changing
  • Beware the Luxury Beach Resort
  • White Author, Black Paragons
  • Why Is Dad So Mad?
  • Should We Blame Social Media for Society’s Divisions?
  • An Ode to My Thesaurus
  • Invitation
Current affairs and politics focussed on the US

entry.title: Scientific American: July 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Record-Breaking Voyager Spacecraft Begin to Power Down
  • Subverting Climate Science in the Classroom
  • How Parents' Trauma Leaves Biological Traces in Children
  • Toxic Slime Contributed to Earth's Worst Mass Extinction--And It's Making a Comeback
  • Astronomers Gear Up to Grapple with the High-Tension Cosmos
  • 'Momentum Computing' Pushes Technology's Thermodynamic Limits
  • Students Deserve to Learn about the Climate Emergency
  • Readers Respond to the March 2022 Issue
  • How Connected Cars Can Map Urban Heat Islands
  • Marker Tip--Without Ink!--Makes a Hardy Medical Sampler
  • New Satellite Is a 'Swiss Army Knife' in Space
  • Dinosaur Diets May Help Explain Dramatic Diversity
  • How Antarctic Krill Coordinate the Biggest Swarms in the World
  • These Spiders Spring Off Their Mates to Avoid Sexual Cannibalism
  • 'Frog Skin' Cell Type Found in Mammal Mouths
  • News Briefs from around the World: July 2022
  • Venomous Snail Unlocks New Diabetes Drugs
  • See the Strange Underground Detector Probing Neutrino Mysteries
  • Skies Are Sucking More Water from the Land
  • How Snakes Breathe while Crushing Prey
  • Crowdfunding Isn't Enough in a Crisis
  • Why Do Mental Illnesses--From Depression to Schizophrenia--Raise the Risk of Dementia?
  • We Need to Make 'Electrifying Everything' Easier
  • Climate Destroyers Go to Jail, Martian Travel Guide, Bee Interiority, and More
  • 50, 100 & 150 Years Ago: July 2022
  • Wildfires Followed by Severe Rain Will Become More Common
  • Poem: 'On Visible Light'
  • Why Kids Are Afraid to Ask for Help
  • Science Needs to Shrink Its Carbon Footprint
Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.
